elements.sent-2411-001: We theoretically investigate the Casimir effect originating from Dirac fields in finite-density matter under a magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, we focus on quark fields in the magnetic dual chiral density wave (MDCDW) phase as a possible inhomogeneous ground state of interacting Dirac-fermion systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this system, the distance dependence of Casimir energy shows a complex oscillatory behavior by the interplay between the chemical potential, magnetic field, and inhomogeneous ground state.
elements.sent-2411-004: By decomposing the total Casimir energy into contributions of each Landau level, we elucidate what types of Casimir effects are realized from each Landau level: the lowest or some types of higher Landau levels lead to different behaviors of Casimir energies.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, we point out characteristic behaviors due to level splitting between different fermion flavors, i.e., up/down quarks.
elements.sent-2411-006: These findings provide new insights into Dirac-fermion (or quark) matter with a finite thickness.
elements.sent-2411-007: The Casimir effect proposed by Casimir~\cite{Casimir:1948dh is of great importance for understanding small-volume physics in quantum field theory.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Casimir predicted that a reduction in the zero-point energy of the photon field by two parallel conducting plates in a vacuum would induce an attractive force, known as the Casimir energy or Casimir force.
elements.sent-2411-009: This theoretical prediction was experimentally confirmed several decades later~\cite{Lamoreaux:1996wh,Bressi:2002fr (for reviews, see Refs.~\cite{Plunien:1986ca,Mostepanenko:1988bs,Bordag:2001qi,Milton:2001yy,Klimchitskaya:2009cw,Woods:2015pla,Gong:2020ttb,Lu:2021jvu ).}}
elements.sent-2411-010: While the original Casimir effect means an attractive force from the photon field in a vacuum, other types of Casimir (or Casimir-like) effects have also been explored.
elements.sent-2411-011: For example, one can consider counterparts induced by fermion fields~\cite{Johnson:1975zp,Mamaev:1980jn such as quarks or in systems filled by a medium~\cite{Dzyaloshinskii:1961sfr .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Such an unusual setup sometimes leads to anomalous phenomena, such as sign-flipping and oscillating behaviors of Casimir energy as a function of distance.
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, in fermionic systems, (i) external magnetic fields\footnote{ Since the photon is not directly coupled to an external magnetic field, the magnetic response of the photonic Casimir effect in quantum electrodynamics (QED) vacuum is described as a higher-order correction due to electron-positron loops~\cite{Robaschik:1986vj .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: On the other hand, the Casimir effect originated from Dirac fields~\cite{Cougo-Pinto:1998jwo,Cougo-Pinto:2001kks,Elizalde:2002kb,Ostrowski:2005rm,Miltao:2008zza,Sitenko:2014kza,Sitenko:2015eza,Sitenko:2015wzd,Nakayama:2022fvh,Rohim:2023tmy,Erdas:2023wzy,Flachi:2024ztd (as well as charged scalar fields~\cite{Cougo-Pinto:1998jun,Cougo-Pinto:1998mdw,Cougo-Pinto:1998fpo,Cougo-Pinto:1998zge,Ostrowski:2005rm,Erdas:2013jga,Erdas:2013dha,Sitenko:2014wwa,Sitenko:2015eza,Erdas:2015yac,Erdas:2020ilo,Haridev:2021jwi, Erdas:2021xvv,Erdas:2024gkq ) is directly affected by magnetic fields.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Therefore, quark fields which we consider in this work can be a realistic testing ground for the Casimir effect coupled to magnetic fields.

The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ and the wave number $\vec{b}$ of density waves are parallel to $z$ the direction. } \label{fig:schematic}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper, we investigate the Casimir effect for Dirac fields under both (i) and (ii) for the first time, where we particularly focus on thin quark matter under a magnetic field (see Fig.~\ref{fig:schematic ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: Our previous work~\cite{Fujii:2024fzy found that an {\it oscillating Casimir effect is induced not only by the quark Fermi sea but also by the dynamics of quark fields modified in the {\it dual chiral density wave (DCDW) phase~\cite{Dautry:1979bk,Tatsumi:2004dx,Nakano:2004cd which is a possible ground state of quantum chromodynamics (QCD) at finite quark chemical potential.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the DCDW phase (more generally, an inhomogeneous phase) can exist only in a narrow density region.
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, in this study, we consider a more feasible situation, that is, the DCDW phase under a magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-020: When a magnetic field is imposed on the DCDW phase, the property of the DCDW is modified, which may be called the {\it magnetic dual chiral density wave (MDCDW) \cite{Frolov:2010wn (see Refs.~\cite{Tatsumi:2014wka,Nishiyama:2015fba,Carignano:2015kda,Ferrer:2015iop,Ferrer:2016toh,Abuki:2018iqp,Ferrer:2019zfp,Feng:2020vxn,Ferrer:2020ulz,Ferrer:2021vuy,Anzuini:2021gwi,Gyory:2022hnv,Ferrer:2022afu,Ghalati:2023npr,Ferrer:2023xvl,Ferrer:2024xwu for related studies and Ref.~\cite{Ferrer:2021mpq for a review).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: A magnetic field makes the DCDW phase more robust, and in particular, at finite temperature, it removes the Landau-Peierls instability~\cite{Ferrer:2019zfp .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Such robustness might support the formation of DCDW in fireballs produced by heavy-ion collision experiments and in the interior of neutron stars.
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-024: In Sec.~\ref{Sec:model , we review the MDCDW phase described by an effective model of interacting Dirac fermions and explain how to calculate Casimir energy in this phase.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In Sec.~~\ref{Sec:result , we show our results.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Sec.~\ref{Sec:summary is devoted to the conclusion.}
elements.sent-2411-027: To investigate quark matter in a magnetic field, we use the Nambu--Jona-Lasinio (NJL) model~\cite{Nambu:1961tp,Nambu:1961fr which is an effective model of QCD (see Refs.~\cite{Vogl:1991qt,Klevansky:1992qe,Hatsuda:1994pi,Buballa:2003qv for reviews).}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The Lagrangian density of the NJL model in a magnetic field is written as \begin{equation}L_{\rm NJL}=\bar\psi(i\slashed{D}+\mu\gamma_0)\psi+G[(\bar\psi\psi)^2+(\bar\psi i\gamma_5\vec{\tau}\psi)^2], \label{eq:NJL}\end{equation} where the quark field $\psi$ has two-flavor ($N_f=2$) and three-color ($N_c=3$) components, and $\mu>0$ is the chemical potential of quarks.
elements.sent-2411-029: The covariant derivative is defined as $\slashed{D \equiv \gamma^\mu(\partial_\mu+iQA_\mu)$ using the gauge field $A_\mu$ and the electric-charge matrix $Q \equiv {\rm diag (q_u,q_d) = {\rm diag (\frac{2 {3 e,-\frac{1 {3 e)$ with the elementary charge $e>0$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Note that, in a vanishing electromagnetic field, the model~(\ref{eq:NJL ) satisfies the $SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$ chiral symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-031: When an external magnetic field is switched on, this symmetry is broken to $U(1)_L\times U(1)_R$.
elements.sent-2411-032: As the mean-field ansatz for the DCDW phase, we adopt \begin{equation} \langle \bar{\psi}\psi \rangle=\Delta\cos(\vec{q}\cdot\vec{r}), \langle \bar{\psi}i\gamma_5 \tau_3 \psi\rangle=\Delta\sin(\vec{q}\cdot\vec{r}), \langle \bar{\psi}i\gamma_5 \tau_1 \psi\rangle=0, \langle \bar{\psi}i\gamma_5 \tau_2 \psi\rangle=0,  \label{eq:MFansatz}\end{equation} where $\Delta$, $\vec{q =(0,0,q)$, and $\vec{r =(x,y,z)$ are the amplitude of DCDW, the wave number of DCDW propagating in the $z$ direction, and the position vector, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Using the mean-field ansatz~(\ref{eq:MFansatz ), we obtain the mean-field Lagrangian, \begin{equation}L_{\rm MF}=\bar\psi[i \slashed{D}+\mu\gamma_0-M(\cos {qz}+i\gamma_5\tau_3\sin{qz})]\psi-\frac{M^2}{4G},\end{equation} where $M=-2G\Delta$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: By performing a local chiral transformation \begin{equation}\psi\rightarrow e^{i\gamma_5\tau_3qz/2}\psi , \ \ \ \bar\psi\rightarrow \bar\psi e^{i\gamma_5\tau_3qz/2}\end{equation} (called the Weinberg transformation), we can eliminate the position dependence in the Lagrangian.
elements.sent-2411-035: The Lagrangian is then rewritten as \begin{equation}L_{\rm MF}=\bar\psi(i\slashed{D}+\mu\gamma_0-M+\gamma_5\tau_3\gamma^\mu q_\mu/2]\psi-\frac{M^2}{4G}. \label{eq:L_MF}\end{equation} Assuming also that the background electromagnetic field is $A_\mu=(0,0,Bx,0)$ using the Landau gauge, we obtain a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ parallel to $\vec{q}$ (see Fig.~\ref{fig:schematic ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: From the Lagrangian (\ref{eq:L_MF ), by diagonalizing the inverse of the quark propagator in momentum space, we obtain the following energy eigenvalues~\cite{Frolov:2010wn : \begin{equation}\omega_{l=0}=E_{l=0}+b-\mu, \ \ \ \tilde{\omega}_{l=0}=-E_{l=0}+b-\mu, \label{EV_LLLs} \omega_{\zeta,l}=E_{\zeta,l}-\mu, \ \ \ \tilde{\omega}_{\zeta,l}=-E_{\zeta,l}-\mu, \label{EV_HLLs} E_{l=0} \equiv \sqrt{M^2+k_z^2}, \notag E_{\zeta,l} \equiv \sqrt{(\zeta\sqrt{M^2+k^2_z}+b)^2+2|q_fB|l}, \ \ \ (l=1,2,...), \notag\end{equation} where we redefined $b_\mu \equiv q_\mu/2 = (0,0,0,q/2) = (0,0,0,b)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The presence of a magnetic field splits the eigenmodes of quarks into an infinite number of Landau levels (LLs) labeled by $l$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The two modes with $l=0$, Eq.~\eqref{EV_LLLs , are called lowest Landau levels (LLLs), and an infinite set of four modes with $l\geq1$, Eq.~\eqref{EV_HLLs , is called higher Landau levels (HLLs).}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The LLLs have no spin index because only one spin component is chosen.\footnote{In the LLLs, Eq.~(\ref{EV_LLLs ), the asymmetry between $\omega_{l=0}$ and $\tilde{\omega _{l=0}$ due to $b\neq 0$ is called the {\it spectral asymmetry ~\cite{Frolov:2010wn,Tatsumi:2014wka .}}}}}
